The Directors of the Great Southern and Western
Railway Co. published their circular at such a time that
it was impossible for us to have anything to say on it

Jast week. The circular is signed by Sir William Gould-
_ing, Baronet, on behalf of the Directors, and we assume
Gir William is the author of it. Sir William is

‘Assurance Company had, a few years ago, taken ac-
tion, and asked for a Committee of Inquiry, we have
1o -doubt that Joseph Todhunter Pim would have been
‘annoyed; and Joseph Todhunter Pim, though not a
aronet, is as highly respectable a man as Sir William
ing. We are not surprised that Sir William
ivectors, including Frederick C. Pil-
., who is also a director of the Na-
urance Co., are annoyed. It is only human
If the mistress of the house pays a surprise
ery, it is human enough that

ENT AFFAIRS:— feel annoyed when common, low-down shareholders of the
The G. S. and W 8 c: Company display an inquiring turn of mind. In the
The Pcb\\lanlb\(D\ir(;cg::-lsllway ... 193 one case, however, the mistresses’ first concern is the
193  health and welfare of the children; in the' other the

shareholders’ concern is the health and welfare of their
property. The petulance of nursery maids and railway’
directors must be taken as part of the day’s work.

We take it from Sir William’s petulant letter that
the directors—nine of whom, by the way, have not, in
the aggregate, as large a stake in the Company as one
particular member of the Sharcholders’ Committee—we
take it that the directors intend to stave off the inquiry ;
They would be wiser, we suggest, if they courted inquiry ;
but they know their own know. If they do not want
an inquiry, they have reasons for their distaste for it ;
and the ordinary shrewd shareholder, putting two and
two together, will not feel any the more confidence in
the concern and its management in consequence of the
anti-inquiry disposition shown by the Board of Direc-
tors. Of course, no one makes any reflection on the
personal integrity of the highly respectable gentlemen
who compose the Board of Directors; but in view of the
awful collapse of the National Assurance Company it
is nob business to expect shareholders to place infallible
trust in the ability of any collection "of men however
high their respectability may be—particularly in view
of the serious depreciation of the property.

Sir William Goulding and his co-directors were so
hard pressed for a reply to the statement made on be-
half of the Shareholders’ Committee that in their des-
peration they tried to drag religious denominationalism
into the matter! Why this dragging of matters con-
nected with religious persuasion into business?  This
is purely a Jusiness matter; and the attempt of the
divectors to draw a denominational red herring across
the trail only shows the desperation to which they have
been driven. Sir William Goulding refers to the fall in
certain other railway company shares. ‘Well, probably
these railways would be none the worse if Shareholders’
Committees of Inquiry looked into their affairs.  Of
course, the shareholders of the Great Southern and
Western Railway ought to feel very thankful that it
was in that railway, and not in the National Assurance
Company, that they had their money ; there was a good
deal, no doubt, in the remark of the man who, on see-
ing his new hat being rolled over by a train, remarked—
«Well, I ought to be very thankful that my head was
not in it.”’ The shareholders of the Great S. and W
Railway could be in a worse plight ; the National As-
surance shareholders have not only lost all their money,
but they are to be mulcted in a big “call’’ to meet
the losses. The shares of other railways have shown a
drop in value as well as the Great S. and W.; these
things are comsoling. Bub the situation is—the pro-
perty of the ordinary shareholders has so considerably
depreciated in value—nearly to the extent of three mil-
lions sterling in a few years—that every consideration
of prudence and business loudly calls for a Committee of
Inquiry into the affairs of the Company. To what-
ever extent those causes that have depreciated the capi-
tal value of Consols may have operated in depreciating
the capital value of the Ordinary Stock of the G.S. and
W. Railway, it is clear that those causes have not had,
and could not have had, any influence whatever on th;
carning powers of the shareholders’ property. — And
the profits have seriously gone down.

Sir William Goulding and the directors have brought

heat, and attempted to drag partisanship, and even sec- ;

tarian prejudice, into this purely business matter. Act-
ing thus, they showed their weakness and their chagrin.

The Dublin Industrial Development Association ap-
p~ars to have taken root, ·and to be growing in a 
vigorous manner. 
A deputation from it waited on the 
Dublin Port and_ Docks Board one day last week. 
Mr. 
George Perry was the principal spokesman of the depu-
tation. He truly said:-" There was, he feared, a cer-
tain amount of indifferentism amongst contractors in 
reference to Irish manufacturers, who, it was to be 
_feared, were not pushful enough in brino-ing their goods 
before the public." Mr. Perry suggested a revisa.l of 
the. ~o.ntract forms sent b1 publ~c bodies and greater

The mdustrial problem is a very 
complex one. 
The idea which prevails to some extent 
even yet am~ngst workmen that t_he less they do the 
more there will be for them to do 1s a fallacy that has 
had a very bad effect on the industrial economy of Ire-
land. As Mr. George ·Perry said:-" But without the 
assistance of the artisans all the efforts of the manu-

attentive and steady, and gave good return for 
the 
money paid-(hear, hear)-they would then all feel in

Mr. Irwin, T.C., in the course of his remarks, as re-

Dublin Industrial Development Association has a great 
career of usefulness before it, and very many of the 
right stamp of men are taking a leading interest in it.

The success of the last Limerick Industrial Exhibition
was 50 encouraging that it is proposed to hold another—
but on a larger scale—during July, 1906. A public
! meeting was held in the Chamber of Commerce with a
§ view to promoting this Munster and Connacht industrial

- cnterprise. The meeting was a very representative one.
Father J. M. Cregan, Adm., to whose energy is to be
credited the St. Itas’ Shirt Factory of Limerick, E. Cole
Welson, James Flynn, City High Sheriff; G. Spillane,
Alderman 8. O’Mara, F. C. Cleeve, J.P.; R. Gibson,
Sir Charles Barrington, M. O’Callaghan, Gerald Good-
body, T. Geary, E. J. Long, M. D. Shaw, David Tid-
marsh, J. S. Gaffney, Solr. ; J. O’Grady Delmege, D.L. ;
Rev. John Lee, C.C.; W. F. M'Namara—these various
classes of men were amongst those present, and their
names indicate the broad and representative basis upon
which the proposed exhibition in July of next year is
to be carried out.

Mr. T. A. Ferguson proposed “that an Industrial
Exhibition be held in Limerick, opening the first week
in July, 1906, to be called ¢ The Munster and Connacht
Industrial Exhibition.”” In supporting this resolution
Mr. James S. Gaffney, Solr., in the course of his re-
marks as reported in the Munster News, said :—“ It was
a great pity that they, Limerick people, and Irish
people as a whole, especially in the South of Ireland,
should not rely sufficiently on themselves, and show that
reliance by buying where they could goods made in Ire-
land that were as good and cheap as goods made else-
Z where (hear, hear). He had adopted the practice in his

own household of buying Irish-manufactured goods.
The crux was, would things be got as good and as cheap
in Ireland as elsewhere, and in that relation he had seen
a most interesting correspondence in a newspaper with
some of the views of which many people did not agree—
he meant the Dublin Leaper, which had done an enor-
mous amount of good for the manufacturers of Ireland
(hear, hear)—a correspondence as to whether people
should buy Irish goods even at prices higher than those
paid for the foreign goods, and he saw that the vast ma-
jority of the manufacturers of Ireland were unanimous
in saying that they did not want any patronage on the
ground that they were Irish manufacturers—they did
not want anybody to buy goods from them except they
- were as good and as cheap as goods manufactured in any
paxt of the world (hear, hear). If they put that forward
in supporting this Exhibition, it would be a success.”’

A Committee consisting of the Executive of the Lime-
k Industrial Association and the following—with
er to add to their numbers—were appointed to carry
he arrangements for the. Munster and Connacht
ition of 1906 :—Sir Thomas Cleeve, Messrs. J. E.

There was a “ Grand Christy Minstrel”’ entertain-
mont and concert at St. Mary’s Temperance Hall, Irish-
town, Clonmel. A programme of the great event is
before us.  The strenuous, manly and self-respecting
majority at a recent meeting of the Executive of the
Gaelic League in Dublin should look after the Clonmel
niggers. They had no Irish vocal items, nay, even
more, they had no Anglo-Irish vocal item at their enter-
tainment at St. Mary’s Temperance Hall, Irishtown,
Clonmel, and yet no “unauthorised persons’’ even
“held up ’* Bones! ' One of the niggers sang “ Looking
for a Coon like me.”” Who was looking for him? As
we are persons ‘“ obnoxious to Irish Ireland,’”’ perhaps
we have no right to intrude our enquiries into such mat-
ters. Why should we ask questions of Coons if we may
not ask a question of the Central Body of the Gaelic
League? There are possibly  unauthorised’’ Coons
as well as “ unauthorised’” Gaelic Leaguers! And if the
Gaelic League authorities are above criticism, why
should not the Coons, also, be exempt? Very Rev.
Canon Flavin, P.P., an Irish speaker, is Director of the
Temperance Sodality, but Canon Flavin is not respon-
sible for this un-Irish programme. We are informed
that Canon Flavin, with his own money acquired two
houses (one of them a drunkery, the licence of which he
extinguished). These two houses he reconstructed, ex-
tended, furnished, fitted and converted into a Tem-
perance Hall for the benefit of his parishioners. The
working men themselves were given a free hand in mak-
ing out the programme of their first concert, and the
innane and unnational thing that we have been refer-
ing to was the result. More than one member of the
Committee attempted to have national songs introduced,
but to no purpose. The performers, all, we understand,
“ patriots ”’ of the greenest type, were more concerned
about showing off their powers as vocalists in nigger and
English lyrics than singing even Anglo-Irish songs like
“The Wearing o’ the Green,”” “ Who fears to speak of
’98,”” or even Moore’s melodies. But as persons ob-
noxious to Irvish Ireland,”’” what business is it of ours?
Some members of the Executive of the Gaelic League
should look after these niggers of Clonmel.

‘We have received a report of the annual meeting of
the Duhallow Coisde Ceanntair which was held recently
at Boherbee. Father P. O’Regan, C.C., Vice-President,
was in the chair. The Secretary’s report was as
follows : —The Coisde was established at a meeting held
in Kanturk, on March 25th, 1904, and has been, there-
fore, something more than twelve months in existence.
During that time eight meetings have been held and
twenty-five resolutions passed, and this represents the
sum {otal of the work of your body. When the Coisde
was established all the six branches of which it consists
were conducting Irish classes; but owing to the want of
a competent teacher among their members, no classes
were conducted in two of these branches during last
session. It will, therefore, be seen that the language
is in a worse condition in Duhallow at present than it
was twelve months ago. Were it not for the action, or
rather inaction, of your Coisde and of the Coisde
Gnotha, these two branches would probably have
managed to have classes somehow. As early
as May last, the question of appointing a
travelling teacher for the Coisde Ceanntair avea was
under  consideration, but was postponed  to
October. When the matter was brought on again at
the October meeting, the majority of the delegates re-
fused to take any action on it, and would not even ask
the Coisde Gnotha for a subsidy towards paying a teacher
for the branches that had none. The Secretary of one
of the branches affected, seeing that no help was to be
expected from your body, explained to the Coisde Gnotha
that his branch would fall through unless they got
some aid from the Central body to help them to pay
the salary of a teacher. To this letter he received no

We have received St. Golumba., the bilingual annual" 
issued· by the League of St. Columba, Maynooth Col-
lege. It is published at one shilling net, by Messrs. 
M. H. Gill and Son, Ltd. 
The object of the League of 
St. Columba is to foster in the students a due appre-
ciation of the val-µe in the cause of Religion, and of the 
consequent claims upon the attention of Ecclesiastics, 
of stuclies--(a) In National Lite.rature and Language ; 
(b) In Na~ional History, especially Ecclesiastical; (c) In 
Irish Hagiology, Archreology, Social Manners; and 
Customs; and to encourage amongst its members the 
cultivation of these various departments, in order that 
they may be more zealous and better qualified to labour 
in extending and controlling move:gients connected with 
those objects, and so perpetuate the truly Religious and 
Catholic Nationality represented .by and identifi~d with 
them. 
The articles, poems, etc., in the Irish language 
which appear in the volume, cover close on forty pages 
in this bulky annual of some 110 pages of reading 
matter. 
Rev. W. O'Kennedy writes on the need of a 
religious literature in the Irish Language. 
We note 
that a book of Irish sermons copied from the MSS. in. 
the library of Maynooth College, and edited by the 
students themselves for the Columban League, will be 
issued next month.

Happily, quite a net-work of Industrial Development

The natural

Irish industry is largely represented at the All Ire- • 
land Temperance Bazaar now proceeding at Hallsbridge. 
In addition to the Irish industries stall, which is ex-
clusively devoted to Irish Manufactures, many other 
stalls have a prominent display of home-made goods. 
The wide constituency to which the Bazaar appeals has 
enabled the raffling system to be eliminated, and_ as

should study the reply signed by Mr. M. A. Sullivan,
LL.B. and Mr. J. Donohoe, J.P., carefully and coolly
as a business document. ~We would remind Share-
holders in the country that their proxies should reach
Mr. Sullivan not later than the first post on to-morrow
(Friday) morning.

A REVIEW OF EDUCATION IN IRELZAND.

ROM the Anglo-Norman invasion to the attempted
“reformation ’’ of the country the education
question in Ireland was a political one only. Such in-
terests as language, laws, manners, national ideals were
involved in it. But the inhabitants of the Pale only
were affected ; for, even in the reign of Henry VIIIL.,
the laws made by the Parliament of the Pale were not
cheyed twenty miles outside Dublin. But the year
1537 marks the beginning of a new epoch in the rela-
tions hetween England and Ireland. The King claimed
spiritual as well as political supremacy, and the attempt
to make the Irish substitute his authority for the spiri-
tual supremacy of the Pope brought a new element into
the education question. It henceforth became a politico-
religious question, and it has remained so to this day.

The object, avowed or concealed, of every system of
cducation which has been devised for the Irish people
cven since has been the Anglicisation of their country.
The introduction of the religious element has not
changed the ultimate purpose. It has been sought to
Protestantise them, not so much to save their souls, as
to Anglicise them. The spiritual authority assumed
by Henry was wrapped up in his royal authority, as the
lesser is” contained in the greater, or as the means is
directed to the end. However the name may be dis-
owned, it is not easy to see how a religion can be na-
tionalised without being Erastian. Had the attempts
to Protestantise the Irish Catholics been wholly or
mainly successful, Ireland should no longer be a nation,
but a province, with no more national individuality
than Yorkshire. The Irish in using one and the same
word, Sspanac, to express Protestant and Englishman,
interpret the philosophy of their history more truly and
thoroughly than most of them have thought.

If we except whatever education Irish Catholics ac-
quired by stealth, from that time to 1782 the stern alter-
native for them was, their faith without education, or
education without their faith. An Act passed in 1665,
during the reign of Charles II., fixed that alternative
by law; and more stringently still, an Act passed in
1695 during the reign of William III., and another
passed in 1703 under Queen Anne. During all the
18th century the law did not suppose a Catholic to re-
ceive an education, since, as was declared officially from
the bench by an Irish judge in 1758, the “law did not
suppose a Papist to exist in the Kingdom, nor could
they breathe without the connivance of government.”’
Catholic education became possible in 1782, but under
certain difficult and dangerous conditions. In 1793 the
first Relief Act came. The clouds of persecution then
began to break, and Catholics were allowed the privilege
of having their own private schools at their own ex-
pense; they were also permitted to graduate in Trinity
College, but without the privilege of its prizes or emolu-
ments. Since that time several systems of education
have been framed for them. But they have not been
allowed to frame any public system for themselves, and
they justly disown all responsibility for educationall
shortcomings and failures. They rightly say; let those.

who have framed those systems which have hitherto been:

tried, presuming to understand better than om‘selves‘
what system suits us best, bear the discredit of failure

changed. Anyone who carefully considers the various
systems as they follow one after another in a sort of
funeral procession, will notice how the methods have
be_eu fined dq\vn gradually from the open assault on
faith and nationhood made by the Parish School Systom
' which was inaugurated in the sixteenth century to the
National System, the Model Schools, and the Queen’s
Colleges in which a system of United Education pro-
fessed to bind the youths of all creeds or of no creed in
bonds of non-sectarian love, in the same non-sectarian
atmosphere, and under the common instruction of the
same noun-sectarian teachers. The most innocent coun-
tenance is proverbially the most dangerous; and the
I:ramc\‘s of this brotherly system would have Catholics
forget that it provides best for those who believe least.
It must, of course, thoroughly satisfy those who have
no belief _at all, or for whom religion is but the subjec-
tive emotion as it comes and passes. Educational secu-
l{lri_sts coolly assume that secularism is not a sect; and
sitting aloft in the serene heaven they naively under-
take the office of impartial arbitrator in composing the
consciences of warring creeds by the arithmetical arti-
fice of a religious common denominator. But what they
really do is to grind the axe of their own creedless sect.
I thus roughly draw out the order of procession.
First, the doctrines retained by Protestantism were to
be taught, and those rejected by it as Romish excres-
cences on the pure Gospel were to be denounced in the
schools. Next, the Bible, to which no Christian could
-object, was to be taught and explained by competent
teachers. Those two having failed, Christian com-
passion for the illiterate condition of the Catholics

would undertake to teach them how to read and write,,

and to bring them up as loyal and godly citizens. The
idea then dawned on our educators that past failure
was due to a serious oversight, inasmuch as they had
‘heen trying to teach the Papists through a language
which these did not understand ; and hence a new edu-
.cational venture was tried, in which the Irish language
was to be the language of the schools. It was to carry
out that idea that a chair of Irish was founded in
Trinity College; although the dons of that Institution,
now that the Gaelic League has become a power in the
country, tell us that patriotism, not proselytism, was
the inspiration, when that system also followed the
failure of its predecessors our educators declared that
“ gyery valley shall be exalted, and every mountain and
hill shall be made low, and the crooked shall become
straight, and the rough ways plain;” they professed to
decree the doom of educational privilege by a National
System of “separate religious and united secular instruc-
tion.”” And lastly, it is now discovered that there has been
something wrong with our education all the time. b
appears to some that priests, monks,‘uuns, aud' Trish
Catholics generally are largely responsible for their un-
practical education and for their consequent economic
condition. They have made themselves responsible, it
is said, by having given a wrong direction to their sys-
tems of education—systems which, be it remembered,
‘they have not framed, but which have been f'mmed for
and forced upon them. Their faith is to be
strengthened, the national idea is to be intensified by a

system which will cultivate ¢ character and morale,
and will generate the civic virtues,” :m‘d “those
strenuous qualities’’ which the non-Catholics of the
North have brought across the Channel. This new idea
has this in common with its predecessors, that the true
inspiration must still be wafted from Holyhead towards

blin Bay. ;

all, it may be true that no nation can know how to edu-
cate another so well as one can educate itself. ~What
then if, as a last resort, after those successive financial
extravagances and educational failures, the Irish people
were allowed to devise a system to educate themselves?
At any rate, if they fail their educators will admit that
the failure will have followed the best example. .

I have before me a list of nearly thirty systems with
objects, dates of institution, regulations, and sources of
revenue, which have been introduced into Ireland since
the 16th century. But I must limit my observations
to a few, and only so far as to illustrate what I have said.

By an Act passed in 1537 (28. Henry VIIL.) parsons
were obliged by oath and a pain of forfeiture of benefice,
amongst other parochial offices in the English interest, “to
keep or cause to be kept within the paroch a schole for to
learne English.”’ That is the origin of what came to
be known as the Parish School system, the object of
which was on the one hand to promote “the English
order, habit and language,”” and to abolish the ‘“manner
and native apparel.” But little was done to carry oub
the scheme till the reigns of Charles, William, and
Anne; and then, according to the Report of the Edu-
cation Commission of 1810, “the advancement of the
Protestant religion was more distinctly provided for **
in them. But the Commissioners also report that in
the vast majority of parishes no schools were kept,
that those which did exist were not free according to
the provisions of their institution, but were maintained
by the fees of the pupils; and finally the Commissioners
condemned them as failures.

In 1570, a system of Intermediate schools, known as
the Diocesan Free Schools, was instituted by the fol-
lowing Act (12th Elizabeth):— Forasmuch as the
greatest number of the people of this your Majesties
realm hath of long time lived in rude and barbarous
states, not understanding that Almighty God hath, by
His divine laws, forbidden the manifold and haynous
offences, which they spare not daily and hourly to com-
mit and perpetrate, nor that he hath by his Holy Scrip-
tures commanded a due and humble obedience from the
people to their princes and rulers; whose ignorance in
these so high pointes touching their damnation, pro-
ceedeth only of lack of good bringing up of the youth of
this realm, either in publique or private schooles, where
through good discipline they might be taught to avoid
those loathsome and horrible errours; it may, therefore,
please your Most Excellent Majestie that it be enacted,
and bee it enacted by your Highnesse, with the assent
of the Lords spirituall and temporall, and the Commons
in this present Parliament assembled, and by the
authority of the same, T'hat there shall be from hence-
forth a free schoole within every diocese of this realm of
Ireland, and that the schoolmaster shall be an English-
man, or of the English birth of this realm, etc.”’ That
Act explains the object of those schools. But although
it provides that they should be provided and main-
tained out of the diocesan revenues, the provision was
carried out only in some dioceses. Hence an Act was
passed in 1755 empowering Grand Juries to levy the cost
of building off the counties. But little heed was paid
to it, and a Royal Commission (1789-1791) in reporting
the neglect, recommended that in addition to a building
grant half the masters’ salaries should also be borne by
the county rates.  Yet, in spite of every artifice to help
on the scheme the Education Commissioners of 1810
doubt “ whether a system should be continued which in
its principle appears not altogether equitable, and has
never been found efficient in practice.”’ Things had

one so badly that the Lord Lieutenant and Council, in
1824, fixed endowments for the maintenance of such
schools as were habitable. But the Education Enquiry
Commission of that year report in May 1827, that al-
though he ordered the salaries for the masters he did
not exercise his power of appointing masters to earn and

The last sum may, in part at least, as we have shown
above, be recovered from persons still living. The re-
mainder is fairly chargeable on the gross property of
the late Established Church which is now vested in the
hands of the Church Commissioners. That property
was held subject to the charge of maintaining Diocesan
Schools as a primary burden. Therefore, the accumu-
lated arrears payable on foot of that charge must in
equity be liquidated out of it. That is to
say :—For the purpose of reconstructing the system of
Intermediate Education in Ireland, there is available an
actual fund connected with Diocesan Schools of £1670
16s. 3d. (or under proper arrangements, of £5,000) a
year, besides a capital sum of accumulated arrears
amounting to £208,795, the interest upon which at 5,
per cent. would be nearly £11,000' a year. In other]
words, were a new, system of Intermediate Education!
in Treland started, there ought to be available for the
purpose of its support an annual income of nearly
£16,000, derived from funds exclusively connected with
the Diocesan Schools of the 12th Elizabeth.””

Dr. Kyle, whom I have already quoted, also stated
before the Royal Commission on Primary Education, on
24th October, 1868, that “ The whole system of Dio-
cesan Schools is perfectly and completely rotten.’’

As part of his Plantation Scheme, James I., in 1608,
set apart 100,000 acres of the confiscated lands for the
endowment of the Protestant Church and the education
of the people of Ulster ; and he ordered that there should
be one Free School at least in each county. That was
the origin of the Royal Free Schools. But we can learn
of the interest taken by his representatives in the edu-
cation even of themselves from the fact that in 1633,
Strafford, the Lord Deputy, complained that “ the lands
which were given to thése charitable uses were dis-
sipated, leased forth for little or nothing; and all the

S moneys raised for charitable uses are converted to pri-
vate benefits.”” Little was done for thirty years more.
The Commissioners of 1789-1791 report on the six Royal
Free Schools then existing, that the master of one had
no scholar; the master of another never kept a school,
in fact, was an absentee; the master of another had,
neither himself nor by proxy, done the duties of school-

master for some years. Later on some signs of life ap-
peared ; but the Reports of the Commissioners of 1854-
1858, and again of 1880-1881, show them as a whole in

a condition of inefficiency and decay. Their net revenue
from lands at the time of the Commission of 1879 was |
£5,463 17s. 8d. They were instituted as Free Schools ;
but although endowed for that purpose they have never
been free. Bver since they have been used at all, they
have been monopolised by middle class Protestants al-
though they should be made available for all.

In 1613, James I. gave to the Irish Society—.c., the
London Companies of Undertakers—a charter of incor-
poration and grants of the City and County of Derry
for, amongst other trusts, the purpose of education; in
the words of the charter, “ to stir up and recall the same
province (of Ulster) from superstition, rebellion, cala-
mity, and poverty, which heretofore have horribly raged
therein, to religious obedience, strength, and pros-
perity.”’  The property in charge of the Society has 2
ben frequently sequestrated for misuse of the trust. %
The surveyor of the Society, in his evidence before the
Education Commission of 1868, set down its gross an-
nual income at £21,059; and from a general statement
of accounts which he presented, it transpires that one-
third of that income held in trust “ to recall (the peopley
from superstition, rebellion, calamity, to religious obe-
dience, strength, and prosperity,”” has been consumed
in the process of management.

In 1657, Erasmus Smith, who had obtained large
tracts of land in several parts of Ireland under the
Cromwellian Settlement, devoted some to the establish-
ment of Free Grammar Schools. The most that can be
said of that trust is that it has produced better educa-
tional results than those already mentioned. ‘The Com-
missioners of 1854-1858 report that the governors
have not only neglected the primary trust of the Gram-
mar Schools, but have not prudently managed the
secondary trust of the English Schools.” Their com-
plaint bears on neglect of inspection, extravagance in
legal expenses, irregular kecping of accounts, and other
such shortcomings.

I pass over the Bluecoat Hospital Free Schools
founded in 1672; several endowed Grammar Schools
founded in the course of the 18th century, after the
manner of those of Erasmus Smith and the Irish Society,
for the “scttlement ’’ of the country; the Foundling
Hospital Schools, instituted in 1704 by the Duke of Ox-
mond for the education of deserted children, maintained
chiefly by Parliamentary grants for the “ English order,
habit, and language’ purpose, ran as reproachful a
career as the Charter Schools, and died for the bemefit
of the country about seventy years ago. The Charter
Schools, the tradition of which is one of injustice,
cruelty, filth, immorality, and ignorance, whose mana-
gers and teachers would have been imprisoned if the
Anti-Cruelty Society had existed in those days; the
Hibernian Military School founded in 1769, and the
Hibernian Marine School in 1775, both maintained out
of the public funds, yet, till recently, appropriated for
the children of Protestant soldiers; and I come to the
Schools founded in 1792 by “The Association for Dis-
countenancing Vice, and Promoting the Knowledge and
Practice of the Christian Religion.””

To my fat position like glue I will cling,
Though critics assail me, and satirists sting.

Let people thin-skinned to retirement go fly ,
When three score and ten’s heavy burden is nigh,
But people like us at their posts will all stay,
While they have the strength to walk up for their pay.
Come, youngsters, come.
The place would go to ruin and to wreck
If from its supervision we remained
For any time away.
Fog., Fos., Rel—Wreck and ruin, wreck and ruin.
Exeunt—Curtain. A M.W.

SOME DINNERS, SOME JUDGES AND A MORAL.

OMEONE remarked of the Catholic Association
that at least it had struck terror into the white
livers of the Ascendancy Party. And the Leaper has
certainly stiffened the backs of the mere Irish Papists and
made them realise that they have at least a right to
exist. You see evidences of this in the unlikeliest places.
Some time since a Conservative candidate was con-
testing Stephen’s Green. Politics aside, he was a de-
cent fellow enough, and he said that his ideal of a har-
monious Ireland was such a mingling of creeds and con-
victions, all with a common purpose of goodwill, as was
found in the coffee room of the Four Courts. Another
sanctuary of legal reputation is the dining hall- at
King’s Inns. At six o’clock each evening, what Byron
calls “ the tocsin of the soul, the dinner bell ’’ sounds,
and the bench, the bar and the budding barristers sit
down to dine.

Scarcely a man of any note in the history of Ireland
during the past century but has passed through this
dining hall. Nearly every one of any eminence has
discussed the burning questions of the day through all
those long years with his neighbour across the table,
between the soup and the snuff. And certainly it is
safe to say that at no other assembly in Ireland has each
side of every question been heard so -intelligently and
so good humouredly. The prevailing tone is perhaps a
little cynical, but where every man’s bad points are
canvassed as clearly as his good qualities, such a tone
will always prevail. There is a cosmopolitanism in the

Raw students from the Royal University and young 
Freshmen from Trinity are check by jo.wl with hardened

The mark of the LEADER influence 
is seen at the grace. 
No man living has been found 
who knows the words of the grace. It is evidently in 
Latin, and its composition is attributed to Erasmus. 
the first word, " Benedicas " wafts solemnity over the 
standing assembly, but the remainder has been for years 
a mystery. 
Now about the half or nearly half, are 
Catholics. 
Opposite sides are not so evenly balanced at 
any other function. 
But some years ago the Oatholic's 
tribute to his Faith was a furtive shamefaced sweep of 
his hand over the region of the abdomen when grace 
was said ; and some of the baser sort did not even do 
so much. But now so much more backbone has been in-
fused into the younger students that they invariably 
bless themselves in a way that is careful, open, deTi-
berate, and even almost ostentatious. 
The " Dark 
Brothers " who used to think it the height of wisdom 
not to pretend that they were Papists, are now shamed 
into some sort of a sign of Catholicity-but largely for 
fear that they should be despised by the Protestants 
present who generally like the man who presents his 
colours.

sionally gives rise to adverse comment. 
They are the 
Di:i M ajores of those functions, and should be models 
to the Catholic students. Some of them omit to cross 
-themselves at the grace, whether from a furtive hope 
that their Catholicity will be forgotten or a despicable 
dread of irritating their neighbours.

place, but once he has attained office he has nothing
